summary: Gruber writes a fictional phone conversation with Dan Lyons (of Fake Steve Jobs fame) about Lyons's 'Dear Gruber: You've Been Pwned' post. The imagined exchange quickly shifts from Lyons checking if Gruber took offense to Lyons venting about his frustrations at Newsweek — the magazine's redesign, shrinking word counts, and having to fawningly interview Mark Zuckerberg. Gruber closes with a dry put-down of Newsweek as the magazine people read when Time is sold out. The piece is pure satirical fiction, mocking both Lyons's dual identity as serious journalist and irreverent blogger, and the declining state of legacy newsweeklies.
key_points: The entire post is a fabricated conversation, turning Lyons's own Fake Steve persona trick back on him
Lyons is portrayed as frustrated by Newsweek's dumbing-down — 100-word 'features' and a USA Today-style redesign
The Zuckerberg interview anecdote skewers both tech media sycophancy and Silicon Valley's casual culture
Gruber's closing line is a perfectly calibrated backhanded compliment that dismisses Newsweek entirely
money_quotes: It's like they turned USA Today into a magazine but left out the good parts, like Larry King's column.
My last piece, my editor tells me he wants 100 words. I go, 'I thought this was supposed to be a feature?' He goes, 'It is.'

I don't think that dopey kid can even make change, he spends the whole interview picking gunk out of his toes through those damn flip-flops, and I'm stuck treating him like he's the second coming of Bill Fucking Gates.
You're writing for Newsweek, the premier magazine Americans turn to for week-old news when they're sold out of Time.
I love my gig at Newsweek, but I can't exactly blow off any steam there. Not much of a taste for humor down there.
key_insight: By writing a fictional conversation in the style of Lyons's own Fake Steve Jobs character, Gruber simultaneously defuses Lyons's jab and delivers a sharper critique of legacy newsweekly journalism's decline.
